dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for hemorrhage/lacerations, 88-year-old female at 4237 Raven Hill in Richmond.
Good afternoon, I'm EMT Mike with Richmond Rescue. What brought you to call 911 today?
I'm her daughter. She has slurred speech and is not acting like herself. I noticed blood in her mouth.
Is this abnormal behavior or level of alertness typical for her?
No, this is completely out of character. She's usually alert and oriented.
Reactive to verbal stimuli, understands basic commands and names.
GCS is 14. E-O-V-M: 4-4-6. She follows commands with both arms and turns toward sound.
Airway is patent and maintainable. Preferred posture is semi-Fowler as already assumed.
Breathing regular and normal in depth. No cyanosis or signs of labored breathing.
Pulse is strong and regular at 45. Skin is warm, dry, and not pale-consistent with patient's appearance. Skin tone appropriate for her ethnicity.
Pulse is 45. Let me check respiration.
Respiratory rate is 14, appears unlabored.
Blood pressure is 105 over 48. I'll check blood glucose now.
Blood glucose is 119. All normal so far.
SpO2 is 96% on room air.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: When did you first notice the slurred speech?
She had normal communication yesterday at 5:36 AM. At 10:30 AM today, I noticed her speech was slurred and asked someone else to confirm.
Has she had any food or water recently?
Not that I know of. She lives alone and usually manages by herself.
Dry blood in the mouth. Eyes and ears are clear. No facial drooping or drooling observed.
Neck is normal. No signs of trauma, stiffness, or swelling.
Chest is clear to auscultation. No crepitus or deformity. Breathing sound is normal and bilateral.
Abdomen is flat and soft. No rigidity or tenderness. Bowel sounds normal.
Back is difficult to assess due to her resting position and clothing. Can she grip my finger?
Squeezes. Looks at hand.
No signs of upper or lower limb dysfunction. No hemiparesis observed. GCS remains 14 but only oriented to person.
Applying 4-lead EKG. Documenting baseline cardiac status.
EKG shows sinus bradycardia at the moment. Initiating 12-lead.
Cincinnati prehospital stroke scale is negative. Facial symmetry and eye movements normal.
12-lead EKG shows abnormal Q waves in aVF/V3-activating protocol 3-5 for potential stroke.
Stroke/TIA protocol 3-5 activated due to altered mental status and abnormal EKG findings.
Rechecking vitals. Pulse is 99, BP is 101/63.
SPO2 now at 97% on room air after initial drop.
Ma'am, can you rate your pain on a scale from 0 to 10?
She says she's feeling no pain at all.
Patient is ready for Reeves stretcher. Maintaining semi-Fowler position en route.
Patient secured on stretcher. Wings up x 2. Ready for boarding.
She's awake and only oriented to person-A&O 3.
Hospital has been alerted. ER is preparing.
SpO2 remains 97%. No signs of oxygen saturation drop during transport.
Does she have any medical conditions or take any medications regularly?
Yes, she has a history of primary hypertension and is on valsartan (Diovan). No known allergies.
No conflicts noted between current vitals and baseline. She's on no new medications.
GCS remains 14. Only oriented to person. For now, no need to repeat Cincinnati stroke test.
Condition stable in transport-no changes in behavior or vitals so far.
Moving smoothly. No movement discomfort.
Transport destination is Memorial Regional Medical Center. Confirmed with hospital.
Re-assessing EKG. Pulse elevated, but sinus rhythm still present.
12-lead remains the same. No acute changes observed.
No new complaints or changes reported. Still no pain and accurate glucose level.
ER is prepared for arrival. All findings and vitals have been relayed by radio.
Patient received 4-lead and 12-lead EKG, GCS 14, A&O 3, no pain, normal glucose.
Used draw sheet assisted movement into the hospital. No further complications noted.
Hospital accepts and accepts transfer of care. ER is ready in room 22.
Patient still in stable condition, successfully moved to bed in ER room 22.
Transfer of care to ER nurse complete. No incidents en route or at transfer.
Care handed off smoothly. No issues reported. Patient remains stable.
All procedures performed per protocol. No issues to note during transport or benching.
Rechecking her orientation. She knows who I am now but not where.
Patient condition remains unchanged from baseline to hospital arrival.
Transferred directly to ER bed using draw sheet. ER nurse assumes care.
No incidents during transport. Patient received full documentation and imaging before arrival.
All last vitals unchanged-BP: 101/63, Pulse: 99, SpO2: 97%.
Final vitals documented. ER is satisfied with inbound review and findings.
All log entries transmitted successfully. No transport incidents to report.
No new findings or concerns reported from ER upon arrival.
EKG monitoring stopped at ER. All findings documented in field.
